history: React (component-based, large ecosystem, quick prototyping) → Node.js/Express with third-party dining/campus API integration (e.g., HotSpot, Tapingo)
loo_notes: React + Node.js/Express is a solid, industry-standard choice with good ecosystem support for rapid prototyping. The plan hinges entirely on third-party API availability (HotSpot, Tapingo)—if those APIs don't exist or require authentication the student doesn't have, the data integration layer collapses; a fallback to mock data or web scraping should be documented now to protect feasibility.

history: React (component-based, large ecosystem, quick prototyping) → Node.js/Express server with scraped dining hall data (HTML parsing or Playwright)
loo_notes: React is the right choice and hits the framework bar, but scraping dining hall data from campus websites in 14 days is a risky dependency—you need to validate that target URLs exist and are scrapable *before* committing. If the scraping fails or the HTML structure changes, your data layer collapses. A safer hybrid would be React + a public dining API (if one exists for your campus) or React + mocked data with a clear path to swap in live data post-deadline.

history: Vue (lighter learning curve, good for rapid development) → Serverless backend (Firebase/Supabase) with pre-loaded or real-time dining data
loo_notes: Vue + serverless is a solid, pragmatic choice for 14 days—you'll move fast and avoid ops overhead. The real question is your data source: if you're pre-loading dining data, you're sidestepping the integration learning objective; if you're syncing real-time from an actual campus API or scraping, this jumps to 0.85+. Clarify upfront whether the data is mocked, live, or scraped, because that commitment makes or breaks feasibility.

history: Plain HTML/CSS/JS (minimal dependencies, simpler deployment) → Public dining API if available (most reliable, but may not exist for target campus)
loo_notes: Vanilla JS sidesteps the core learning objective of component architecture and state management — you'll spend 14 days rebuilding patterns that React or Vue give you for free, leaving almost no time for the real challenge: integrating live dining data. The public API bet is also risky; if it doesn't exist, your whole plan collapses with no fallback.

history: Plain HTML/CSS/JS (minimal dependencies, simpler deployment) → Mock/hardcoded dataset (full control, no external dependencies, faster development)
loo_notes: Vanilla JS sidesteps the core learning objective—no component architecture, state management, or modern tooling means students miss critical industry skills. Hardcoded data compounds the problem: it's not a real external data source, so you're building a static site, not a dining app that teaches integration.
